turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Breyer has the opinion of the Court in case 16-1519, Lagos v. United States.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: The petitioner Sergio Fernando Lagos was convicted of defrauding General Electric Capital Corporation of millions of dollars. When GE found out that it was being defrauded, it began its own investigation and spent about $4 million on lawyers, accountants and then another million on legal fees in a bankruptcy proceeding. The question in this case is whether GE can recover those costs from the petitioner Lagos under a provision of the Mandatory Victims Restitution Act that requires defendants who are convicted of certain federal crimes, like his, to reimburse their victims for "lost income and necessary childcare, transportation and other expenses incurred during participation in the investigation or prosecution of the offense or attendance at proceedings related to the offense." We hold that the statute, a particular one, does not cover the types of expenses that Lagos challenges. In our view the critical words of the statute, investigation, proceeding refer to government criminal investigations, prosecutions, and proceedings, not to the private investigations and noncriminal proceedings in which GE became involved. We rest our conclusion primarily upon the wording of the Act. The Act refers to "the investigation or prosecution of the offense", which suggests that a government's criminal investigation and criminal proceedings are at issue. This is further supported by the types of expenses that the Act enumerates, lost income, childcare, transportation, those are the kinds of expenses that a victim participating in a government investigation and attending criminal proceedings would more normally expect to incur and they are not the main kinds of expenses that a victim conducting a private investigation or pursuing a civil or bankruptcy litigation would expect to incur. For these reasons and various others, quite a few others, that we set forth in our opinion we find the restitution provision, that particular one, inapplicable to the types of expenses that Lagos challenges. We reverse the contrary conclusion of the Fifth Circuit. We remand the case for further proceedings consistent with our opinion. The Court's decision is unanimous.
